name: Tab Smith
abstract: Talmadge "Tab" Smith (January 11, 1909 – August 17, 1971) was an American swing and rhythm and blues alto saxophonist. He is best remembered for the tracks "Because of You" and "Pretend".

about_quotes.speaker: Teddy Wilson
about_quotes.evidence: Tab was on alto, but he came to my big band, and he did a good job sitting in Ben's chair playing all the solos that Ben had played and meeting the part.

about_quotes.speaker: David Albert Francis
about_quotes.evidence: So he said, 'Would you like to join my group?', so I told him yes... And then myself on drums. Saxaphones were Tab Smith...

about_quotes.speaker: William Randolph "Cozy" Cole
about_quotes.evidence: The interviewer mentions Tab Smith in the context of musicians Cozy Cole would have seen or known in the scene ('See all those boys were all good riff men... Tab Smith made a great little name for himself'), but the interviewee does not explicitly confirm a personal interaction, collaboration, or cl

about_quotes.speaker: Buck Clayton
about_quotes.evidence: And they were in good shape. Maybe it was because they knew that they were running up against Basie. And we thought, we'd took them lightly. This was at the time they, didn't they have Tab Smith and Billy Kyle? Yes. Well, I mean, that was a pretty good band.

about_quotes.speaker: James Moody
about_quotes.evidence: There was, I mean, there was like Tab Smith (phonetic sp.) with Count Bassie, but I, you know, like I kind of narrowed it down because all these guys they play nice saxophone, but that wasn't the way I wanted to play.
about_quotes.doc_title: James Moody — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Hubert Laws
about_quotes.evidence: I heard that saxophone sound. Boy, I was so enamored with Because of You... I was saving up my money to buy an alto saxophone so I could emulate that sound from Tab Smith.
about_quotes.doc_title: Hubert Laws — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History
